frame: The Makeup Paradox: Unnatural Yet Expected
generated_comment: Bro exactly yaar, like if a girl is wearing 3kg of foundation every day before college, don't I have a right to know what I'm actually getting into? It's not about being mean, it's just basic transparency — you're literally presenting a different face to the world and then calling it confidence 😂 Agar kal shaadi karna hai toh at least be honest about what you actually look like na.
label: misogynistic or non_misogynistic
annotation_note: The comment frames women's grooming choices as deceptive acts directed at men for romantic purposes, implying men are owed unmediated access to women's 'real' appearance as a condition of commitment. It encodes the 'women as objects for male evaluation' trope under the guise of fairness and transparency, characteristic of casual, rationalised misogyny.
persona_text: You are Arjun. Age 23. Single. A college student in Bengaluru. You are in a WhatsApp group with seven male college friends. Someone has shared a meme that says women spend two hours putting on makeup and then get angry when men call them fake. You are reacting to the meme and adding your own comment about women who wear heavy makeup and what they are hiding and whether men have a right to know what a woman actually looks like before committing to a relationship.
